title: Longest Palindrome After At Most K Replacements
description: Max palindrome length with limited replacements

examples.explanation: String: abca, K=1. Substring from index 0 to 3 ('abca'): mismatches between position 1 ('b') and 2 ('c') = 1. With 1 replacement (e.g., change 'c' to 'b' → 'abba' or 'b' to 'c' → 'acca'), it becomes a palindrome of length 4. This is the maximum.
String length=1. Only substring (0,0)='a'. Check palindrome: left=0, right=0, while left<right is false (0<0 false), so mismatches=0 ≤K=0. Length=1. So max_len=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Check all possible substrings for palindrome validity by counting mismatches and comparing to K, using nested loops to generate substrings and verify each. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution for Longest Palindrome After At Most K Replacements
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all substrings for palindrome validity with limited replacements using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to find the longest palindrome substring achievable with at most K character replacements. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible substring of the input string, check if it can be transformed into a palindrome with ≤K replacements, and track the maximum length found. The algorithm works by generating all substrings using two nested loops (for start and end indices), then for each substring, we check palindrome validity by comparing characters from both ends inward. We count mismatches where characters differ, and if mismatches ≤K, the substring qualifies. We track the maximum length among qualifying substrings. The time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) substrings and each palindrome check takes O(n) time in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: 1) Not resetting mismatch count for each substring, 2) Off-by-one errors in substring indices, 3) Forgetting to handle empty strings, 4) Not considering that replacements can make any character match any other. To avoid these, carefully manage loop boundaries and reset counters. Real code pattern: def longestPalindrome(s, K): max_len = 0; for i in range(len(s)): for j in range(i, len(s)): mismatches = 0; left = i; right = j; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: mismatches += 1; left += 1; right -= 1; if mismatches <= K: max_len = max(max_len, j-i+1); return max_len

examples.trace: String: abca, K=1. Substring (0,3)='abca': mismatches between positions 1 and 2 = 1 ≤K=1, length=4 qualifies after one replacement.
String length=1. Only substring (0,0)='a'. Check palindrome: left=0, right=0, while left<right is false (0<0 false), so mismatches=0 ≤K=0. Length=1. So max_len=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if a Substring Can Be a Palindrome with ≤K Replacements
ladders.desc: Function to determine if a given substring can become palindrome with at most K character replacements.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force solution. Given a substring defined by start and end indices, we check if it can be transformed into a palindrome by replacing at most K characters. The intuition is that a palindrome requires symmetric characters to match. We compare characters from both ends moving inward. Each mismatch indicates that the two characters differ, and to make them match, we need to replace at least one of them (in worst case, one replacement per mismatch). Thus, if the number of mismatches is ≤K, the substring can become a palindrome. The algorithm: initialize mismatch count = 0, set left = start, right = end. While left < right, compare s[left] and s[right]. If they differ, increment mismatch count. Then move left rightward and right leftward. After loop, if mismatches ≤K, return true (substring can be palindrome), else false. Time complexity is O(length of substring) which is O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being called for each substring in the nested loops. Common mistakes: 1) Counting mismatches incorrectly by not updating pointers, 2) Not handling odd-length palindromes correctly (middle character doesn't need pairing), but our while loop condition left < right automatically handles this as middle character is not compared. 3) Assuming each mismatch requires 2 replacements; actually, changing one character to match the other fixes it with 1 replacement. To avoid, carefully increment mismatch only when characters differ. Real code pattern: def canBePalindrome(s, start, end, K): mismatches = 0; left = start; right = end; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: mismatches += 1; left += 1; right -= 1; return mismatches <= K

examples.trace: Substring 'abca' from indices 0 to 3. Initialize mismatches=0. left=0, right=3. Compare s[0]='a' vs s[3]='a': equal, so no mismatch. Update left=1, right=2. Compare s[1]='b' vs s[2]='c': not equal, mismatches=1. Update left=2, right=1. Now left=2, right=1, condition left<right is false (2<1 false). Loop ends. mismatches=1. Check if ≤K=1: 1≤1 true. So return true.

Substring 'abc' from indices 0 to 2. Initialize mismatches=0. left=0, right=2. Compare s[0]='a' vs s[2]='c': not equal, mismatches=1. Update left=1, right=1. Now left=1, right=1, condition left<right is false (1<1 false). Loop ends. mismatches=1. Check if ≤K=0: 1≤0 false. So return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Mismatches in a Potential Palindrome
ladders.desc: The technique of comparing symmetric characters in a string to count how many pairs differ.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating from both ends of a string towards the center, comparing characters at symmetric positions, and counting how many pairs are mismatched. The intuition is that for a string to be a palindrome, every character must match its mirror counterpart. By counting mismatches, we determine how many changes are needed to achieve this symmetry. The algorithm: initialize a counter = 0. Use two pointers, left at start index and right at end index. While left < right, compare the characters at these positions. If they are different, increment the counter. Then move left one step right and right one step left. Continue until pointers meet or cross. The final count is the number of mismatched pairs. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the main operation within the palindrome checking subroutine. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to move pointers after comparison, causing infinite loop. 2) Using ≤ instead of < in loop condition, which for even-length strings would compare middle pair twice, but actually for even length, when left and right cross, loop stops; using left <= right would compare extra. But since we compare pairs, left < right is correct as when left == right, it's the middle character (odd length) which doesn't need pairing. 3) Not initializing counter to 0. To avoid, ensure pointer updates and correct loop condition. Real code pattern: def countMismatches(s): mismatches = 0; left = 0; right = len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: mismatches += 1; left += 1; right -= 1; return mismatches

examples.trace: String: a b c a. Initialize mismatches=0. left=0 (a), right=3 (a). Compare: a == a, so no mismatch. Update left=1, right=2. Compare: b != c, so mismatches=1. Update left=2, right=1. Now left=2, right=1, condition left<right is false. Loop ends. Return mismatches=1.

String: r a c e c a r. Initialize mismatches=0. left=0 (r), right=6 (r): match. left=1, right=5: a vs a match. left=2, right=4: c vs c match. left=3, right=3: condition left<right is false (3<3 false). Loop ends. Return mismatches=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Generating every possible contiguous substring using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating all possible substrings of a given string. A substring is defined by a start index and an end index, with characters taken consecutively from the original string. The intuition is that to examine all substrings, we need to consider every possible starting point and every possible ending point that is at or after the start. The algorithm: let n be length of string. Use an outer loop for start index i from 0 to n-1. For each i, use an inner loop for end index j from i to n-1. This generates substrings from index i to j inclusive. The total number of substrings is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). Time complexity for generation is O(n²) if we just iterate indices, but if we also process each substring, it adds more time. Space complexity is O(1) for indices only. This connects to L0 as the way to generate substrings for palindrome checking. Common mistakes: 1) Setting j from i+1 instead of i, missing single-character substrings. 2) Using j < n instead of j <= n-1, but in Python-style range(i, n) gives j from i to n-1, correct. 3) Not handling empty string; for empty string, n=0, loops don't run, which is fine as no substrings. To avoid, ensure inner loop starts at i. Real code pattern: n = len(s); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substring = s[i:j+1] # or just use indices i and j

examples.output: Substrings: a, ab, abc, b, bc, c
examples.trace: String length=3. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j from 0 to 2. j=0: substring indices 0-0 = 'a'. j=1: indices 0-1 = 'ab'. j=2: indices 0-2 = 'abc'. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j from 1 to 2. j=1: indices 1-1 = 'b'. j=2: indices 1-2 = 'bc'. Outer loop i=2: inner loop j from 2 to 2. j=2: indices 2-2 = 'c'. Total 6 substrings.
String length=1. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j from 0 to 0. j=0: substring indices 0-0 = 'a'. Total 1 substring.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Characters for Equality
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of checking if two characters are the same.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two characters to see if they are equal. In programming, characters are typically represented as single-character strings or ascii values. The intuition is simple: we need to determine if two symbols match, which is essential for palindrome checking where symmetric characters must be identical. The algorithm: given two characters char1 and char2, return true if they are the same, false otherwise. In most languages, this is done using equality operator (==). Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the core comparison in mismatch counting. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing characters case-sensitively when not intended, but in this problem, we assume case matters unless specified. 2) Using assignment (=) instead of equality (==). 3) Not considering that characters might be digits or other symbols, but comparison works the same. To avoid, use correct operator and ensure data types match. Real code pattern: def areEqual(c1, c2): return c1 == c2

examples.trace: Compare 'a' and 'a': they are the same character, so return true.
Compare 'b' and 'c': they are different characters, so return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence of characters that reads the same forward and backward. Examples include words like 'racecar' or numbers like '12321'. The intuition is symmetry: the first character matches the last, the second matches the second-last, and so on. For odd-length palindromes, there is a middle character that doesn't need a pair. For even-length, every character has a mirror counterpart. This concept is foundational because our problem revolves around finding or creating palindromes. To check if a string is a palindrome, we can compare characters from both ends. If all pairs match, it's a palindrome. If not, it's not. Time complexity to check is O(n) by iterating halfway. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the goal of mismatch counting. Common mistakes: 1) Thinking palindromes must be words; they can be any sequence. 2) Forgetting that single characters are trivially palindromes. 3) Confusing with other patterns like anagrams. To avoid, remember the definition and test with examples. Real code pattern: def isPalindrome(s): left = 0; right = len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True

examples.trace: Check palindrome: compare index 0 (r) vs 6 (r): match. Index 1 (a) vs 5 (a): match. Index 2 (c) vs 4 (c): match. Index 3 (e) is middle, no pair needed. All comparisons match, so it's a palindrome.
Check palindrome: compare index 0 (h) vs 4 (o): not match, so immediately return false. Not a palindrome.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use DP with a 2D table to store palindrome status and mismatches for substrings, iterating over lengths and indices to compute results based on inner substrings. This reduces redundant checks compared to brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Palindrome After At Most K Replacements
ladders.desc: Complete DP solution to find maximum palindrome length with limited character replacements
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest palindromic substring achievable by replacing at most K characters. The DP approach uses a 2D table dp[i][j] to store the minimum replacements needed to make substring s[i:j+1] a palindrome. The intuition is that we can build solutions for longer substrings from shorter ones. For any substring s[i:j], if s[i] == s[j], then dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] (no extra replacements needed). If they differ, dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 1 (we need to replace one character). We then find the maximum length where dp[i][j] ≤ K. The algorithm iterates over substring lengths from 1 to n, computing dp values bottom-up. Time complexity is O(n²) for filling the DP table, and space is O(n²) for storing the table. This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking logic with replacement counting. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (length 1 substrings need 0 replacements) and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[n][n] with 0 on diagonal; for length from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, j = i+length-1, compute dp[i][j] based on s[i] and s[j]; track max length where dp[i][j] ≤ K.

examples.trace: String: abca, K=2. n=4. Initialize dp 4x4 with zeros. Length 1: dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][1]=0, dp[2][2]=0, dp[3][3]=0. Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=a, s[1]=b differ → dp[0][1]=dp[1][0]+1=0+1=1 (since i+1>j-1, treat as 0). i=1,j=2: s[1]=b, s[2]=c differ → dp[1][2]=1. i=2,j=3: s[2]=c, s[3]=a differ → dp[2][3]=1. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=a, s[2]=c differ → dp[0][2]=dp[1][1]+1=0+1=1. i=1,j=3: s[1]=b, s[3]=a differ → dp[1][3]=dp[2][2]+1=0+1=1. Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=a, s[3]=a match → dp[0][3]=dp[1][2]=1. Now check dp ≤ K: dp[0][3]=1 ≤ 2, length=4 → max=4. Also dp[0][2]=1 ≤2 length=3, dp[1][3]=1 ≤2 length=3, etc. Maximum length is 4.

String: abcde, K=1. n=5. Initialize dp 5x5. Length 1: all dp[i][i]=0. Length 2: i=0,j=1: a≠b → dp[0][1]=1; i=1,j=2: b≠c → dp[1][2]=1; i=2,j=3: c≠d → dp[2][3]=1; i=3,j=4: d≠e → dp[3][4]=1. Length 3: i=0,j=2: a≠c → dp[0][2]=dp[1][1]+1=0+1=1; i=1,j=3: b≠d → dp[1][3]=1; i=2,j=4: c≠e → dp[2][4]=1. Length 4: i=0,j=3: a≠d → dp[0][3]=dp[1][2]+1=1+1=2; i=1,j=4: b≠e → dp[1][4]=dp[2][3]+1=1+1=2. Length 5: i=0,j=4: a≠e → dp[0][4]=dp[1][3]+1=1+1=2. Check dp ≤ K=1: dp[0][2]=1 length=3, dp[1][3]=1 length=3, dp[2][4]=1 length=3 are valid. Also length 2 substrings with dp=1. Maximum length is 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Minimum Replacements for Palindrome Substring
ladders.desc: DP function to compute minimum replacements needed to make any substring a palindrome
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dp[i][j], the minimum character replacements required to transform substring s[i:j+1] into a palindrome. The intuition is based on the palindrome property: a string is a palindrome if its first and last characters match and the inner substring is also a palindrome. For s[i:j+1], if s[i] == s[j], then no replacement is needed for these endpoints, and dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] (with base case dp[i+1][j-1] = 0 when i+1 > j-1). If s[i] != s[j], we need at least one replacement to make them match, so dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. The algorithm fills a 2D table bottom-up by increasing substring length. Time complexity is O(n²) as we iterate over all O(n²) substrings. Space is O(n²) for the DP table. This feeds into L0 by providing the replacement counts needed to check against K. Common mistakes include not handling the base case for length 2 substrings correctly (when i+1 > j-1, dp[i+1][j-1] should be treated as 0). Real code pattern: Initialize dp[n][n] with 0; for length from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, j = i+length-1, if s[i]==s[j] then dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1] else dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1]+1.

examples.input: s = "abca", compute dp for all substrings

ladders.title: Core Logic — Palindrome DP Recurrence Relation
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation that defines minimum replacements based on endpoint characters
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] if s[i] == s[j], else dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. This relation captures the essence of transforming a substring into a palindrome. The intuition is that for any substring, making it a palindrome requires ensuring the first and last characters match. If they already match, we only need to worry about the inner substring (i+1 to j-1). If they don't match, we must replace one of them (incrementing the replacement count by 1) and then handle the inner substring. The base case is when i >= j (empty or single-character substring), which requires 0 replacements. This recurrence reduces the problem to smaller subproblems, enabling DP. Time and space analysis for computing this relation for a single (i,j) pair is O(1), but overall O(n²) when applied to all pairs. This feeds into L1 by being the computational rule for filling the DP table. Common mistakes include incorrect handling when i+1 > j-1 (should return 0) or misindexing. Real code pattern: if i >= j: return 0; if s[i]==s[j]: return dp[i+1][j-1]; else: return dp[i+1][j-1] + 1.

examples.trace: For substring s[0:4] = "abca": s[0]=a, s[3]=a are equal → use case s[i]==s[j]. Compute dp[0][3] = dp[1][2]. Now for dp[1][2]: s[1]=b, s[2]=c are not equal → use else case: dp[1][2] = dp[2][1] + 1. Since i=2 > j=1, dp[2][1] is base case 0. So dp[1][2] = 0 + 1 = 1. Thus dp[0][3] = 1.
For substring s[0:3] = "abc": s[0]=a, s[2]=c are not equal → use else case: dp[0][2] = dp[1][1] + 1. dp[1][1] is base case (single character) = 0. So dp[0][2] = 0 + 1 = 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking if a String is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determine if a given string reads the same forwards and backwards
ladders.explanation: This building block involves checking whether a string is a palindrome, which is fundamental to the DP approach. A string is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backwards, meaning s[i] == s[n-1-i] for all i. The intuition is that we can compare characters from both ends moving inward. The algorithm uses two pointers: one at the start (i) and one at the end (j). While i < j, if s[i] != s[j], return false; otherwise, increment i and decrement j. If all comparisons pass, return true. Time complexity is O(n) for a string of length n, as we check up to n/2 pairs. Space is O(1) as we only use pointers. This feeds into L2 by providing the basic check that the DP recurrence aims to achieve with replacements. In the DP context, we extend this to count mismatches rather than just returning true/false. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings (which are palindromes) or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: i=0, j=len(s)-1; while i<j: if s[i]!=s[j]: return False; i+=1; j-=1; return True.

examples.trace: String: racecar, length 7. i=0 (r), j=6 (r): match. i=1 (a), j=5 (a): match. i=2 (c), j=4 (c): match. i=3 (e), j=3: i not < j, stop. All matched, so true.
String: hello, length 5. i=0 (h), j=4 (o): h != o → return false immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Characters
ladders.desc: Check if two characters from a string are equal
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two characters s[i] and s[j] from a string to see if they are equal. It is a fundamental step in palindrome checking and the DP recurrence. The intuition is simple: characters are equal if they have the same value. In programming, this is typically done using the equality operator (==). The algorithm accesses the characters at indices i and j in the string and returns true if s[i] == s[j], false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time comparison. Space is O(1) as no extra memory is needed. This feeds into L3 by being the core comparison in the palindrome check loop. In the DP approach, this comparison determines whether replacements are needed. Common mistakes include comparing indices instead of characters or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: if i < len(s) and j < len(s): return s[i] == s[j]; else handle error.

examples.trace: String: apple. s[0] = 'a', s[4] = 'e'. Compare 'a' == 'e'? false.
String: banana. s[1] = 'a', s[3] = 'a'. Compare 'a' == 'a'? true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences of characters with indices
ladders.explanation: A string is a fundamental data structure representing a sequence of characters. In programming, it is typically stored as an array of characters, where each character has an index starting from 0. For example, in string s = "hello", s[0] = 'h', s[1] = 'e', s[2] = 'l', s[3] = 'l', s[4] = 'o'. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. Strings are immutable in many languages, meaning you cannot change individual characters directly, but you can access them. The intuition is that strings allow us to represent text and manipulate it character by character. This concept feeds into L4 by providing the basis for accessing and comparing characters. In the context of the problem, we need to understand how to traverse and compare parts of a string. Common mistakes include confusing string length with last index (last index is length-1) or treating strings as mutable without proper methods. Real code pattern: s = "example"; len_s = len(s); first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[len_s-1].

examples.trace: String: apple has length 5. Indices: 0 → 'a', 1 → 'p', 2 → 'p', 3 → 'l', 4 → 'e'. To access first character: s[0] returns 'a'. To access last character: s[4] returns 'e'.
String: cat has length 3. Indices: 0 → 'c', 1 → 'a', 2 → 't'. s[1] is 'a'. The last index is 2 (length-1).

testCases.input: s = "test", what is the last index?
s = "data", what is the first character?

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Two Pointers O(n^2)
approaches.summary: For each center (character or between characters), expand outward with two pointers while counting mismatches and stopping when replacements exceed K, tracking the maximum length. This exploits palindrome symmetry to reduce checks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Palindrome with K Replacements via Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Find the longest palindrome substring achievable by replacing at most K characters in a given string.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to leverage palindrome symmetry to efficiently explore all possible centers (characters or between characters) and expand outward using two pointers. For each center, we simulate expansion while counting mismatches between characters at symmetric positions. When mismatches exceed K, we stop expanding from that center. This brute-force exploration of all centers yields O(n²) time complexity but is optimal for this problem as we must consider all possible substrings. The key insight is that by fixing a center and expanding symmetrically, we only need to check pairs of characters, reducing redundant checks compared to checking every substring independently. The algorithm iterates through 2n-1 possible centers (n character centers and n-1 between-character centers). For each center, it initializes left and right pointers and expands outward while the substring remains within bounds. It counts mismatches (when s[left] != s[right]) and stops when mismatches exceed K. The maximum length encountered across all centers is tracked. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case when K is large enough to allow full expansion from each center. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. Connection to L1: This full solution calls the expandFromCenter helper function repeatedly. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to handle both character and between-character centers, 2) Not resetting mismatch count for each center, 3) Continuing expansion after exceeding K without breaking. Real code pattern: def longestPalindrome(s, K): n = len(s); max_len = 0; for center in range(2*n-1): left = center // 2; right = left + center % 2; mismatches = 0; while left >= 0 and right < n: if s[left] != s[right]: mismatches += 1; if mismatches > K: break; current_len = right - left + 1; if current_len > max_len: max_len = current_len; left -= 1; right += 1; return max_len

examples.trace: String: abca, K=1. Centers: 0 to 7 (2*4-1=7). Center 0 (char a): left=0, right=0, mismatches=0, len=1, max=1. Expand: left=-1 out. Center 1 (between a,b): left=0, right=1, s[0]=a != s[1]=b, mismatches=1, len=2, max=2. Expand: left=-1 out. Center 2 (char b): left=1, right=1, mismatches=0, len=1, max=2. Expand: left=0, right=2, s[0]=a != s[2]=c, mismatches=1, len=3, max=3. Expand: left=-1 out. Center 3 (between b,c): left=1, right=2, s[1]=b != s[2]=c, mismatches=1, len=2, max=3. Expand: left=0, right=3, s[0]=a != s[3]=a? No, same, mismatches=1, len=4, but mismatches=1 <= K=1, so continue, max=4? Wait, len=4 but we need to check if palindrome: abca with 1 replacement? Actually, with K=1, we can replace one char to make palindrome. But our algorithm counts mismatches: at left=0,right=3: match, so mismatches still 1. But to form palindrome, we might need to replace. However, our algorithm assumes we replace mismatched chars. For abca, with center between b,c, we have mismatches=1 at left=1,right=2. Then at left=0,right=3, match, so total mismatches=1, which is <=K, so we consider this substring of length 4. But is "abca" with 1 replacement a palindrome? Replace c with b: abba, yes palindrome of length 4. So max should be 4. Let me recalc: For center 3: left=1,right=2, mismatches=1 (b!=c), len=2. Expand to left=0,right=3: s[0]=a, s[3]=a, match, mismatches still 1, len=4, max becomes 4. So output 4. But example says output 3? I need to correct. Actually, for s="abca", K=1, longest palindrome? Let's think: We can get "aba" by replacing c with b, length 3. Or "abba" by replacing c with b and? Wait, "abca" to "abba": replace c with b, but then we have abba, which is palindrome, length 4. So yes, 4 is possible. But is it? String abca, indices 0,1,2,3. Replace index 2 (c) with b: abba. Yes palindrome length 4. So max is 4. But my algorithm gave 4. So example output should be 4. Let me trace properly: Center 3: between index1 and2. Start left=1,right=2, s[1]=b, s[2]=c, mismatch, mismatches=1, len=2, max=2. Expand left=0,right=3, s[0]=a, s[3]=a, match, mismatches=1, len=4, max=4. So output 4. I'll correct example. Input: s="abca", K=1. Output: 4. Trace: Centers 0-7. Center0: len=1. Center1: len=2 with 1 mismatch. Center2: expand to len=3 with 1 mismatch. Center3: expand to len=4 with 1 mismatch. Others shorter. Max=4.

String: abcde, K=2. Centers: 0 to 9. Center 0 (a): len=1. Center 1 (between a,b): left=0,right=1, a!=b, mismatches=1, len=2. Expand left=-1 out. Center 2 (b): left=1,right=1, len=1. Expand left=0,right=2, a!=c, mismatches=1, len=3. Expand left=-1 out. Center 3 (between b,c): left=1,right=2, b!=c, mismatches=1, len=2. Expand left=0,right=3, a!=d, mismatches=2, len=4. Expand left=-1 out. Center 4 (c): left=2,right=2, len=1. Expand left=1,right=3, b!=d, mismatches=1, len=3. Expand left=0,right=4, a!=e, mismatches=2, len=5. But mismatches=2 <= K=2, so continue? Actually, at left=0,right=4, mismatches=2, len=5, so max could be 5? But is "abcde" with 2 replacements a palindrome? Replace d with b and e with a: abcba, yes length 5. So max should be 5. Let me check: For center 4, start at c, expand to b and d: b!=d, mismatches=1, len=3. Expand to a and e: a!=e, mismatches=2, len=5. So max=5. But example says 3? I need to correct. Actually, with K=2, we can get length 5. So output 5. I'll correct. Input: s="abcde", K=2. Output: 5. Trace: Center4 gives len=5 with 2 mismatches. Others give shorter. Max=5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Expand from Center with Mismatch Counting
ladders.desc: Given a center in a string, expand outward symmetrically while counting character mismatches until exceeding a limit.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find palindromic substrings from a fixed center, we can use two pointers moving outward, comparing characters at symmetric positions. Each mismatch represents a character that would need replacement to form a palindrome. By counting mismatches and stopping when they exceed K, we efficiently determine the longest possible palindrome from that center without unnecessary checks. This sub-routine is the core of the O(n²) solution, called for each center. The algorithm takes the string s, center index (represented as an integer from 0 to 2n-2), and K. It computes left and right pointers: left = center // 2, right = left + center % 2. For odd centers (center % 2 == 1), this represents expansion between characters, starting with adjacent indices. It then enters a while loop that continues as long as left >= 0 and right < len(s). In each iteration, it compares s[left] and s[right]. If they differ, it increments a mismatch counter. If mismatches exceed K, it breaks out of the loop. Otherwise, it calculates the current substring length as right - left + 1 and updates a maximum length tracker if needed. Then it moves pointers outward: left -= 1, right += 1. The function returns the maximum length found for this center. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in worst case when expansion goes to string ends, but on average it's less. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called 2n-1 times in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of symmetric expansion. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect pointer initialization for between-character centers, 2) Not breaking immediately when mismatches exceed K, 3) Off-by-one errors in length calculation. Real code pattern: def expandFromCenter(s, center, K): n = len(s); left = center // 2; right = left + center % 2; mismatches = 0; max_len = 0; while left >= 0 and right < n: if s[left] != s[right]: mismatches += 1; if mismatches > K: break; current_len = right - left + 1; if current_len > max_len: max_len = current_len; left -= 1; right += 1; return max_len

examples.trace: Center=3 (between index1 and2). n=4. left=3//2=1, right=1+3%2=1+1=2. mismatches=0, max_len=0. Iteration1: left=1, right=2, in bounds. s[1]=b, s[2]=c, mismatch, mismatches=1. mismatches=1 <= K=1, so continue. current_len=2-1+1=2, max_len=2. Update pointers: left=0, right=3. Iteration2: left=0, right=3, in bounds. s[0]=a, s[3]=a, match, mismatches still 1. current_len=3-0+1=4, max_len=4. Update pointers: left=-1, right=4. Loop ends as left out of bounds. Return max_len=4.

Center=2 (char index1). n=3. left=2//2=1, right=1+2%2=1+0=1. mismatches=0, max_len=0. Iteration1: left=1, right=1, in bounds. s[1]=b, s[1]=b, match, mismatches=0. current_len=1-1+1=1, max_len=1. Update pointers: left=0, right=2. Iteration2: left=0, right=2, in bounds. s[0]=a, s[2]=c, mismatch, mismatches=1. mismatches=1 <= K=1, so continue. current_len=2-0+1=3, max_len=3. Update pointers: left=-1, right=3. Loop ends. Return max_len=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Symmetric Expansion with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Use two pointers moving outward from a center to check palindrome properties by comparing symmetric characters.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that palindromes are symmetric around a center. By starting at a center and moving two pointers outward equally, we can efficiently check if a substring is palindrome-like (allowing for mismatches). This technique reduces the problem from checking all O(n²) substrings independently to exploring each center and expanding, which is more efficient because it exploits symmetry. The core logic involves initializing left and right pointers at or around the center, then in a loop, comparing characters at these pointers. If they match, the substring remains potentially palindromic; if not, we note a mismatch. The pointers are moved outward symmetrically (left--, right++) until we hit string boundaries. This process naturally checks all substrings centered at that point. The key insight is that by fixing the center, we only need O(n) comparisons per center instead of O(n²) for all substrings. This logic is fundamental to many palindrome problems. Time complexity for expanding from one center is O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the expandFromCenter function with added mismatch counting. Connection to L3: This builds upon the concept of two-pointer traversal. Common mistakes: 1) Moving pointers asymmetrically, 2) Not handling even-length palindromes (between-character centers), 3) Continuing expansion after finding a non-palindrome without the K allowance. Real code pattern: left = center; right = center; while left >= 0 and right < n: if s[left] != s[right]: # handle mismatch; current_len = right - left + 1; left -= 1; right += 1;

examples.trace: Center=3 (char 'e'). left=3, right=3. Compare s[3]=e with itself, match. Move left=2, right=4. Compare s[2]=c and s[4]=c, match. Move left=1, right=5. Compare s[1]=a and s[5]=a, match. Move left=0, right=6. Compare s[0]=r and s[6]=r, match. Expansion completes, substring "racecar" is palindrome.

For even-length palindrome. Option1: center=1 (between index0 and1). left=0, right=1. Compare s[0]=a and s[1]=b, mismatch if strict palindrome, but for expansion logic, we note mismatch. Move left=-1 out. Option2: center=2 (between index1 and2). left=1, right=2. Compare s[1]=b and s[2]=b, match. Move left=0, right=3. Compare s[0]=a and s[3]=a, match. Expansion gives palindrome "abba".

testCases.expected: Palindrome length 1 from char center, but for between-center, length 2
Not full palindrome, max length 1 from center

ladders.title: Building Block — Two-Pointer Traversal from Center
ladders.desc: Initialize two pointers at a center and move them outward in opposite directions while staying within array bounds.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to use two indices that start at the same or adjacent positions and move symmetrically outward. This is a common technique for exploring symmetric structures like palindromes. The building block involves setting left and right pointers based on a given center index. For odd-length palindromes (centered on a character), both pointers start at the same index. For even-length palindromes (centered between characters), pointers start at adjacent indices. The traversal continues as long as both pointers are within the string boundaries (left >= 0 and right < n). In each step, we can perform operations (like comparing characters), then move pointers: left decrements by 1, right increments by 1. This ensures they move outward equally. This block is fundamental because it allows us to examine all substrings centered at a given point without redundant checks. Time complexity for a single traversal is O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This traversal is the mechanism for symmetric expansion. Connection to L4: It relies on basic pointer manipulation and bounds checking. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect initialization for even/odd centers, 2) Not updating pointers symmetrically, 3) Infinite loops if termination condition is wrong. Real code pattern: left = start_left; right = start_right; while left >= 0 and right < len(s): # do something; left -= 1; right += 1;

examples.trace: Start: left=2, right=2. Iteration1: left=2, right=2 (within bounds). Perform op. Update: left=1, right=3. Iteration2: left=1, right=3. Update: left=0, right=4. Iteration3: left=0, right=4. Update: left=-1, right=5. Loop ends as left out of bounds.
Start: left=1, right=2. Iteration1: left=1, right=2. Update: left=0, right=3. Iteration2: left=0, right=3. Update: left=-1, right=4. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Characters and Move Pointers
ladders.desc: Compare two characters in a string and update indices to move outward.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation in the palindrome expansion process. The intuition is that to check for palindrome symmetry, we need to compare characters at symmetric positions and then adjust our positions to check the next pair. The operation involves three steps: 1) Access characters at indices left and right in the string s using s[left] and s[right]. 2) Compare these characters for equality (or other conditions). 3) Update the indices: decrement left by 1 and increment right by 1 to move outward. This operation is repeated in a loop until boundaries are reached. It's basic because it uses simple array indexing and arithmetic. Time complexity per operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed in each iteration of the two-pointer traversal. Connection to L5: It relies on understanding string indexing and comparison. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong indices (off-by-one), 2) Not checking bounds before accessing, 3) Updating pointers in wrong order. Real code pattern: if s[left] == s[right]: # match case; else: # mismatch case; left -= 1; right += 1;

examples.trace: Step1: Access s[2]='l' and s[2]='l'. Compare: equal. Step2: Update left to 1, right to 3.
Step1: Access s[1]='o' and s[3]='l'. Compare: not equal. Step2: Update left to 0, right to 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access characters in a string by index and compare them for equality.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite concept. Strings are sequences of characters, and each character has an index starting from 0. To access a character at a specific index, we use notation like s[i] in many programming languages. Comparison of characters checks if they are the same character; for example, 'a' == 'a' is true, but 'a' == 'b' is false. This foundation is essential because the palindrome problem revolves around comparing characters at different positions. The intuition is that by looking at individual characters, we can build up to checking larger patterns. The operation involves: given a string s and an integer index i, retrieve s[i]. Then, given two indices i and j, retrieve s[i] and s[j] and compare them using equality operator (==). This is typically an O(1) operation in terms of time complexity, and uses O(1) space. Connection to L4: This is used in the compare characters step. Common mistakes: 1) Assuming 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), 2) Comparing strings instead of characters, 3) Index out of bounds errors. Real code pattern: char1 = s[i]; char2 = s[j]; if char1 == char2: # do something;

examples.trace: Step1: Access s[0] = 'a'. Step2: Access s[4] = 'e'. Step3: Compare 'a' == 'e'? False.
Step1: Access s[1] = 'a'. Step2: Access s[3] = 'a'. Step3: Compare 'a' == 'a'? True.

approaches.name: Optimized Sliding Window with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Binary search on the answer length and use a sliding window with a frequency array to check feasibility for each length by counting mismatches in O(n) time. This decouples length search from palindrome verification.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) for frequency array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Palindrome After At Most K Replacements
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search on answer length with sliding window verification
ladders.explanation: This problem asks: given a string s and integer k, find the longest palindrome substring achievable by replacing at most k characters. The optimized approach uses binary search on the answer length L, then verifies if any substring of length L can be made into a palindrome with ≤k replacements. For each candidate length L, we slide a window of size L across s. For each window position, we count character frequencies. The key insight: to make a window palindrome with minimal replacements, we need to make pairs of characters match. For each character, we need to replace half of its occurrences (rounded down) to match the other half. The total replacements needed = (window length - sum of floor(freq[i]/2)*2)/2. This counts mismatches in potential palindrome pairs. We check all windows of length L in O(n) time using sliding window with frequency updates. Binary search reduces total checks to O(log n) iterations, giving O(n log n) overall. The algorithm: 1) Binary search L from 1 to n. 2) For each L, slide window, maintain freq array. 3) For each window, compute needed replacements = (L - sum(floor(freq[i]/2)*2))/2. 4) If any window needs ≤k replacements, L is feasible. Return max feasible L. Space: O(1) for freq array (26 letters). Common mistakes: forgetting to handle odd-length palindromes correctly (the formula accounts for this), not updating freq efficiently when sliding window (add new char, remove old char), incorrect binary search bounds. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution.

examples.trace: n=4, binary search L from 1 to 4. Check L=3: windows: "abc" freq={a:1,b:1,c:1}, sum floor(freq/2)*2 = 0, replacements=(3-0)/2=1.5→2 (ceil), >k=2? 2≤2 true, but need all windows? Next window "bca" same. L=3 feasible. Check L=4: window "abca" freq={a:2,b:1,c:1}, sum floor(freq/2)*2 = floor(2/2)*2=2 + floor(1/2)*2=0 + floor(1/2)*2=0 = 2, replacements=(4-2)/2=1, ≤2, feasible. Max L=4.

n=5, binary search L from 1 to 5. Check L=4: windows: "abcd" freq={a:1,b:1,c:1,d:1}, sum=0, replacements=(4-0)/2=2 >1, not feasible. Next windows similar. L=3: windows: "abc" freq={a:1,b:1,c:1}, replacements=(3-0)/2=2>1? Wait compute: (3-0)/2=1.5→2 ceil, >1, not feasible? Let's check all: "abc":2, "bcd":2, "cde":2 all >1. L=2: windows: "ab": freq={a:1,b:1}, sum=0, replacements=(2-0)/2=1 ≤1 feasible. So max L=3? Actually L=3 not feasible, L=2 feasible. But output 3? Let's recompute L=3 carefully: For "abc", need 2 replacements to make palindrome (e.g., "aba" or "cbc"), >1. So L=3 not feasible. Thus max L=2. But example says 3, maybe mistake. Let's use correct: s="abcde", k=1, longest palindrome with ≤1 replacement is length 2 (e.g., "aa" from "ab" with 1 replacement). So output 2. I'll correct: output 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sliding Window Feasibility Check for Fixed Length
ladders.desc: Check if any substring of given length L can be made palindrome with ≤k replacements in O(n) time
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step: given string s, length L, and k, determine if there exists a substring of length L that can be transformed into a palindrome with at most k character replacements. We use a sliding window approach with a frequency array of size 26 (for lowercase letters). Initialize freq array for first L characters. For each window position i from 0 to n-L, compute needed replacements. The formula: replacements = (L - sum(floor(freq[ch]/2)*2)) / 2. Intuition: In a palindrome, characters must appear in matching pairs (except possibly one middle character). For each character count c, we can form floor(c/2) pairs. The total paired characters = sum(floor(freq[ch]/2)*2). The unpaired characters = L - total_paired. To make palindrome, we need to replace half of these unpaired characters (since each replacement fixes two positions). Thus replacements = unpaired/2. This automatically handles odd lengths: if L is odd, one unpaired character is allowed as center. We check if replacements ≤ k for any window; if yes, return true. When sliding window, update freq: decrement count for s[i], increment for s[i+L]. Time: O(n) per check. Space: O(1). Common mistakes: integer division (use integer math), not updating freq correctly, missing the ceil for odd unpaired (but formula with division by 2 works since unpaired is even? Actually unpaired might be odd, but replacements should be ceil(unpaired/2). Our formula uses integer division, so for odd unpaired, (L - total_paired) is odd, division by 2 truncates, giving floor. But we need ceil. Example: L=3, total_paired=0, unpaired=3, replacements should be 2 (ceil(3/2)), but (3-0)/2=1 in integer math. So correct: replacements = (L - total_paired + 1) / 2? Let's derive: need to replace ceil(unpaired/2). Since unpaired = L - total_paired, and total_paired is even (sum of even numbers), so unpaired has same parity as L. If L odd, unpaired odd, ceil(unpaired/2) = (unpaired+1)/2. If L even, unpaired even, ceil(unpaired/2)=unpaired/2. So formula: replacements = (L - total_paired + (L%2)) / 2. But simpler: compute pairs = sum(freq[ch]//2), total_paired = 2*pairs, unpaired = L - total_paired, replacements = (unpaired + 1) // 2? Wait: for L=3, pairs=0, total_paired=0, unpaired=3, replacements should be 2, (3+1)//2=2 correct. For L=4, pairs=0, unpaired=4, replacements=2, (4+1)//2=2? Actually need 2, yes. But check L=4, pairs=2 (e.g., freq a=4), total_paired=4, unpaired=0, replacements=0, (0+1)//2=0 correct. So replacements = (L - 2*pairs + 1) // 2. Use this. Connection: This sub-routine is called in binary search loop for each candidate L.

examples.trace: n=4, freq for first 3 chars "abc": {a:1,b:1,c:1}, pairs = 1//2 + 1//2 + 1//2 = 0, total_paired=0, unpaired=3, replacements = (3+1)//2 = 2, ≤2 true, so return true immediately.

n=5, check all windows of length 4. First window "abcd": freq {a:1,b:1,c:1,d:1}, pairs=0, unpaired=4, replacements=(4+1)//2=2 >1. Slide: "bcde" similar, replacements=2. All windows have replacements=2 >1, so return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Minimal Replacements for Palindrome in Fixed Substring
ladders.desc: Compute minimal character replacements needed to make a given substring palindrome using frequency counts
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: given a substring (via its character frequencies), compute the minimum number of character replacements required to transform it into a palindrome. The intuition stems from palindrome structure: a palindrome reads same forwards and backwards, so for each position i, character at i must match character at L-1-i. Thus, we need to form matching pairs. For each character, if it appears c times, we can pair up floor(c/2) of them, leaving c mod 2 unpaired. The total number of paired characters = sum over all chars of floor(c/2)*2. The unpaired characters = total length - total_paired. To fix mismatches, each replacement can change one character to match another, effectively fixing two positions at once (by making them equal). Therefore, minimal replacements = ceil(unpaired / 2). Since each replacement reduces unpaired count by 2 (if we replace an unpaired char to match another), we need half as many replacements. But careful: if unpaired is odd, one character can remain as center of odd-length palindrome, so we need replacements for the remaining even unpaired. Thus formula: replacements = (unpaired + 1) // 2, using integer division. Alternatively, compute pairs = sum(c//2), then replacements = (L - 2*pairs + 1) // 2. Example: substring "aab" has freq a:2, b:1, L=3. pairs = 2//2 + 1//2 = 1+0=1, total_paired=2, unpaired=1, replacements=(1+1)//2=1. Indeed, change b to a to get "aaa". Time: O(alphabet size) per substring. Space: O(1). Common mistakes: forgetting the +1 in formula for odd unpaired, not using integer division correctly, misunderstanding that replacements count character changes, not position pairs. Connection: This logic is used inside sliding window to evaluate each window quickly.

examples.trace: Compute pairs: a:2//2=1, b:1//2=0, c:1//2=0, total pairs=1, total_paired=2*1=2, unpaired = 4-2=2, replacements = (2+1)//2 = 1. Explanation: string like "aabc" can become "abba" by changing c to b (1 replacement).
pairs: each 1//2=0, total pairs=0, total_paired=0, unpaired=4, replacements=(4+1)//2=2. Need 2 replacements, e.g., change c to a and d to b to get "abba".

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window with Frequency Array
ladders.desc: Maintain character frequencies for a sliding window of fixed length across a string
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves efficiently tracking character counts for all substrings of a given length L as we slide a window across the string. We use a frequency array freq of size 26 (assuming lowercase English letters). Initialize freq by counting characters in the first L characters of string s. Then, for each step from i=0 to n-L-1, we move the window one position to the right: we decrement the count for character s[i] (the character leaving the window) and increment the count for character s[i+L] (the character entering the window). This update takes O(1) time per slide, allowing us to process all windows in O(n) time total, rather than O(n*L) if we recomputed frequencies from scratch each time. The intuition is that adjacent windows share L-1 characters, so only two characters change. This technique is crucial for problems requiring analysis of all substrings of fixed length. Implementation details: map characters to indices (e.g., ord(ch)-ord('a')), handle edge cases like L > n. Time: O(n) for sliding across entire string. Space: O(1) for fixed-size array. Common mistakes: not resetting freq between different L checks, incorrect index handling leading to out-of-bounds, forgetting to update freq for both remove and add. Connection: This building block enables the feasibility check to run in O(n) time by quickly obtaining freq for each window without rescanning.

examples.trace: Initialize freq for first 2 chars "ab": freq[a]=1, freq[b]=1. Window at i=0: freq {a:1,b:1}. Slide to i=1: remove s[0]='a', add s[2]='c', freq becomes {a:0,b:1,c:1}. Slide to i=2: remove s[1]='b', add s[3]='a', freq becomes {a:1,b:0,c:1}.
First window "aa": freq[a]=2. Slide: remove s[0]='a', add s[2]='a', freq[a] becomes 2-1+1=2. So freq remains {a:2} for all windows.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Frequency Counting in Substring
ladders.desc: Count occurrences of each character in a given substring using an array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves tallying how many times each character appears within a specific substring of a string. We use a frequency array, typically of size 26 for lowercase English letters, initialized to zeros. For each character in the substring, we map it to an index (e.g., using ord(ch) - ord('a') for 'a' to 'z'), and increment the corresponding count. This provides a histogram of character distribution, which is essential for many string analysis tasks. The intuition is that by summarizing the substring into counts, we can derive properties like whether it can be rearranged into a palindrome (which depends on counts of characters with odd frequencies). Implementation: allocate array freq[26] = {0}, loop through substring characters, for each ch, idx = ch - 'a', freq[idx]++. Time: O(L) for substring length L. Space: O(1) fixed array. Common mistakes: not resetting freq between counts, incorrect mapping for non-lowercase characters, off-by-one errors in substring bounds. Connection: This operation is the basis for the frequency array used in sliding window; initially we compute freq for first window, then update efficiently.

examples.trace: Initialize freq array zeros. Process 'a': idx=0, freq[0]++ → 1. 'b': idx=1, freq[1]++ → 1. 'c': idx=2, freq[2]++ → 1. 'a': idx=0, freq[0]++ → 2. Result: freq[0]=2 (a), freq[1]=1 (b), freq[2]=1 (c), others 0.
Process 'a': freq[0]=1, 'a': freq[0]=2, 'a': freq[0]=3. All other indices remain 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Palindrome Property and Character Pairs
ladders.desc: Understand that a palindrome requires symmetric character matching, achievable by forming pairs
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, such as "radar" or "abba". The core property is symmetry: for a string of length L, the character at position i must equal the character at position L-1-i for all i from 0 to floor((L-1)/2). This means characters form pairs: each character on the left side must match a character on the right side. If we think in terms of character counts, in a palindrome, at most one character can have an odd count (which would be the middle character in an odd-length palindrome). All other characters must have even counts, because they appear in matching pairs. For example, in "abba", counts are a:2 (even), b:2 (even). In "abcba", counts are a:2 (even), b:2 (even), c:1 (odd). To transform any string into a palindrome, we need to ensure this property holds. The minimal changes involve making characters match by replacing some characters with others. Intuitively, each replacement can fix two positions at once by making them equal. This foundational idea leads to the strategy of counting pairs: for each character, we can pair up floor(count/2) of its occurrences, and the unpaired characters determine how many replacements are needed. Time/space: conceptual, no specific algorithm. Common mistakes: confusing palindrome with other patterns, not considering odd vs even length. Connection: This concept underpins the formula for computing minimal replacements in higher levels.

examples.trace: Check symmetry: position 0='a' matches position 3='a', position 1='b' matches position 2='b'. All pairs match, so palindrome. Character counts: a:2 (even), b:2 (even), no odd counts (except if length odd).
Check: position 0='a' vs position 2='c' mismatch. Not symmetric. Counts: a:1 (odd), b:1 (odd), c:1 (odd) — more than one odd count, so cannot be palindrome without changes.

testCases.expected: not palindrome (but can be rearranged to abba, which is palindrome)
